diagrams.title: C++ Compilation Pipeline
diagrams.description: A horizontal flowchart showing the four stages of C++ compilation: preprocessing, compilation, assembly, and linking. Arrows connect each stage left to right, with box icons representing each transformation step and output file types.
elements: Stage labels and file extension labels
diagrams.purpose: Helps beginners understand that compilation is a multi-step process, not a single magic operation. Each stage has a specific purpose: preprocessing handles directives, compilation creates assembly, assembly creates object code, and linking combines object files into a runnable executable.

diagrams.title: Source Code to Executable File Transformation
diagrams.description: A vertical or diagonal flow diagram showing the file transformations with icons representing different file types. Shows .cpp input, intermediate .h/.hpp headers being included, .o object files being created, and final .exe/.out output.
elements: main.cpp file icon with C++ code snippet
Object file (.o) icon showing binary representation
Executable (.exe) icon with computer running symbol
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the concrete file transformations that happen during compilation, showing that source code becomes binary executable code. Demonstrates how multiple source files and headers combine into a single executable.

diagrams.title: Program Execution Flow
diagrams.description: A three-panel diagram showing: 1) Writing code in an editor, 2) Compiling with g++/clang++, 3) Running the executable. Shows the terminal/command line inputs and outputs for each step.
elements: Code Editor panel showing source code
Terminal window showing 'g++ main.cpp -o program' command
Terminal window showing compilation success message
Arrow from compiled program to execution
Computer memory diagram showing program loaded into RAM
diagrams.purpose: Connects the abstract compilation process to the practical workflow students use daily. Shows the exact commands and expected outputs at each stage, reinforcing that compiling produces a file that can be run separately from the source code.
